title: GCAP Gets Its Billions, Europe Scrambles to Fill Gaps as US Pulls Back
subhead: The UK, Italy, and Japan award a £4.6 billion contract for the GCAP sixth-generation fighter; NATO's top commander says Europe has largely backfilled US cutbacks; Ukraine's deep-strike drone campaign surges 1,150%; and the MQ-28 Ghost Bat flies with an F-15EX in a milestone for unmanned combat aviation.

sections.heading: GCAP Fighter Jet Secures £4.6 Billion Development Contract
sections.body:
The UK, Italy, and Japan awarded a £4.6 billion ($6.14 billion) contract to the trinational joint venture Edgewing to advance the Global Combat Air Programme (GCAP) sixth-generation fighter jet. The 18-month contract, running from 1 July 2026 through 31 December 2027, covers the completion of the advanced concept and assessment phase and further detailed design and development. It follows an initial £686 million contract placed in April 2026.

The UK's Defence Investment Plan confirmed £8.6 billion in UK funding for GCAP over the next four years. Edgewing — the joint venture formed by BAE Systems, Leonardo, and Japan Aircraft Industrial Enhancement Co — serves as the sole prime contractor and design authority. GCAP, launched in 2022, aims to deliver a next-generation stealth fighter by 2035, replacing the Eurofighter Typhoon in RAF service and Japan's F-2 fleet.

The contract award comes just weeks after the rival Franco-German-Spanish FCAS sixth-generation fighter program collapsed, with Germany and Spain launching 'Team Gen 6' as an alternative pathway. The juxtaposition is striking: GCAP is accelerating with real money and institutional momentum, while Europe's other next-gen fighter effort has fragmented.
sections.whyItMatters: GCAP is now the only operational sixth-generation fighter program in the Western alliance with committed multi-billion funding and a unified industrial structure. The FCAS collapse means Europe's future combat air capability increasingly hinges on a UK-Italy-Japan axis rather than a Franco-German one — a realignment with long-term implications for transatlantic defense industrial politics.

sections.heading: NATO Commander: Europe Has 'Largely Filled' Gaps From US Military Cutbacks
sections.body:
NATO's top commander, Gen. Alex Grynkewich, told the Associated Press that European allies have 'largely filled the gaps' created by the US decision to reduce military contributions to the alliance's Force Model. The US had announced on June 3 that it would no longer supply an aircraft carrier, support ships, aerial refueling planes, and dozens of fighter jets to the continent.

Grynkewich said European nations stepped up 'in a matter of weeks,' and that in the few areas where like-for-like replacements aren't available, NATO is looking at 'alternate capabilities with matching effect.' He provided no specifics on those alternatives.

The statement comes days before the NATO summit in Ankara on July 7-8, where force planning and burden-sharing will dominate the agenda. Reuters reports that NATO leaders including Trump are set to reaffirm an 'ironclad commitment' to collective defense under Article 5. But behind the communique language, the War on the Rocks analysis platform argues that Trump's approach is not genuine retrenchment but 'illiberal hegemony' — extracting higher rents for continued US protection rather than actually withdrawing.

The Pentagon has reduced US forces in Europe from about 100,000 to roughly 80,000, cancelled a long-range fires battalion deployment to Germany, and pulled a brigade combat team rotation from Poland before Trump reversed that specific move. Germany, for its part, is permanently deploying the 45th Panzer Brigade to Lithuania — the first permanent overseas German military deployment since World War II — with plans to grow from 1,600 to 5,000 troops by end of 2027.
sections.whyItMatters: Grynkewich's assessment is a significant data point: it suggests European NATO members can move faster than critics assumed when forced to. But the 'largely' qualifier masks real gaps — particularly in enablers like aerial refueling, intelligence, and strategic lift — that no European substitute fully replicates. The Ankara summit will test whether this improvised backfill hardens into a sustainable posture or remains a stopgap.

sections.heading: Ukraine's Deep-Strike Drone Campaign Surges 1,150% in 2026
sections.body:
Ukraine's Unmanned Systems Forces reports a 1,150% increase in deep-strike drone operations inside Russia since the start of 2026, with 172 military-industrial and energy targets hit in June alone. The figures, reported by Euromaidan Press and confirmed by MSN, reflect a dramatic scaling of Ukraine's indigenous long-range drone manufacturing and operational capabilities.

On the defensive side, Ukraine unveiled a new mini air defense system designed to shoot down FPV drones and other low-altitude threats, including a platform called 'Peace Duck' for interceptor missions. Ukraine's defense ministry also said it is buying 100 Patriot interceptor missiles using $1 billion in EU loans, and has issued 40 letters to allies requesting urgent Patriot missile stocks. President Zelensky has sought a US license to produce anti-ballistic missiles domestically.

Russia, meanwhile, has been forced to slash fuel quality standards to Euro-3 levels as Ukrainian drone strikes squeeze domestic refinery output, according to the Kyiv Independent. Western intelligence also reports that Russia has fitted its Black Sea submarines with anti-drone cages to protect against Ukrainian strikes — a remarkable adaptation for vessels that were once considered beyond the reach of drone warfare. The Russian Black Sea Fleet command is reportedly planning relocation from Crimea to Novorossiysk, according to headlines, though this has not been independently confirmed.
sections.whyItMatters: The 1,150% figure is staggering but reflects an industrial transformation, not just tactical escalation. Ukraine has built a domestic drone industry that can sustain hundreds of deep strikes per month, forcing Russia to degrade its own fuel standards and harden submarines with ad-hoc cage armor. The Patriot procurement scramble underscores the asymmetry: Ukraine can make drones but still can't make the interceptors it needs to defend its cities.

sections.heading: MQ-28 Ghost Bat Flies With F-15EX in Pacific Milestone for Manned-Unmanned Teaming
sections.body:
The Boeing MQ-28 Ghost Bat collaborative combat aircraft flew together with an F-15EX Eagle II during Exercise Valiant Shield 2026 over the Philippine Sea — the first known manned-unmanned teaming flight between the two platforms. PACAF confirmed the milestone, stating that 'uncrewed systems act as a force multiplier, extending the reach and effectiveness of human pilots.'

The MQ-28, designed by Boeing Australia, also conducted a proof-of-concept Forward Arming and Refueling Point (FARP) operation for collaborative combat aircraft on June 28, supported by an HC-130J and personnel from the Royal Australian Air Force and the US Air Force's Experimental Operations Unit (EOU). The EOU was activated in June 2025 at Nellis AFB to accelerate CCA integration.

The same F-15EX subsequently deployed to Kadena Air Base, Japan, on June 29 — its second familiarization visit there as the base transitions from F-15C/Ds to the new Eagle IIs. The 67th Fighter Squadron will be Kadena's first operational F-15EX unit, with 36 new fighters expected to be delivered starting next year.
sections.whyItMatters: The Ghost Bat-F-15EX integration isn't a lab demo — it's happening in a major Pacific exercise with Australian participation. This is the most tangible evidence yet that the US Air Force's Collaborative Combat Aircraft concept is moving from development toward operational employment, with real implications for Pacific force posture and the future of air combat.

sections.heading: RENK Acquires David Brown Defence, Consolidating Naval Propulsion Market
sections.body:
German propulsion specialist RENK Group AG has entered a binding agreement to acquire David Brown Defence (DBD), the Huddersfield-based manufacturer of gearboxes for the Type 26 frigate and other major naval programmes, from private equity firm Stellex Capital Management. The transaction is expected to close in Q4 2026; commercial terms were not disclosed.

DBD carries an order backlog and pipeline worth over £700 million for 2026-2030, supplying main propulsion transmission systems for the Global Combat Ship family — covering up to 34 vessels across the Royal Navy's Type 26, Australia's Hunter class, and Canada's River class destroyers. RENK gains submarine propulsion expertise, UK land programmes including Challenger 2 and Boxer, and access to the Five Eyes defence market. RENK generated approximately EUR 1.4 billion in revenue in fiscal year 2025.

The UK's National Armaments Director Rupert Pearce endorsed the deal, calling it 'a strong endorsement of the UK's industrial base.' RENK CEO Dr. Alexander Sagel said the acquisition continues the group's strategy of strengthening its defense sector position through targeted M&A.
sections.whyItMatters: The RENK-DBD deal consolidates the Western naval propulsion market under fewer players at a time when submarine and surface combatant programmes are scaling across NATO. That the UK's top armaments official publicly endorsed a German acquisition of a strategic UK supplier signals how much European defense industrial policy has shifted toward integration over national champions.

sections.heading: KNDS IPO Delay Highlights European Defense Industry Strains Amid Rearmament
sections.body:
Franco-German tank maker KNDS has delayed its planned initial public offering, Reuters reports, underscoring what the outlet calls 'growing unease over the defence industry's ability to keep pace with Europe's rearmament' demands. The German government says it assumes KNDS will still pursue the listing, but the pause reflects market volatility and investor caution.

The KNDS delay is the latest sign that Europe's defense industrial base — tasked with producing everything from artillery shells to next-generation tanks — faces a capacity and capital problem. Demand has surged since Russia's invasion of Ukraine, but the industry's supply chains, workforce, and production lines remain sized for a peacetime era. The Kiel Institute recently concluded that European NATO members remain 'strategically dependent on the United States across the entire military chain of operations,' despite collectively spending more on defense than any country bar the US.

Germany plans to reach 3.5% of GDP defense spending by 2029, well ahead of the 2035 deadline NATO is expected to set. But money alone won't solve the structural bottlenecks — skilled labor shortages, fragmented procurement, and decades of underinvestment in production capacity remain the binding constraints.
sections.whyItMatters: Europe's defense spending promises are colliding with industrial reality. KNDS makes the Leopard 2 and other platforms that are central to NATO's ground force posture, and its IPO was supposed to raise capital for capacity expansion. If the flagship European defense listing can't clear the market, it raises hard questions about whether the continent's defense industrial base can scale fast enough to match its political ambitions.

sections.heading: South Korea Eyes NATO Defense Market as Lee Heads to Ankara Summit
sections.body:
South Korean President Lee Jae Myung will attend the NATO summit in Ankara on July 7-8 and then make a state visit to Mongolia, Seoul announced. It will be Lee's first NATO leaders' meeting since taking office in June 2025. Lee will deliver keynote remarks at the NATO Defense Industry Forum during the 'Shared Values, Stronger Industrial Foundations' session.

Seoul's stated goal is to 'establish a full-scale foothold for advancing into the world's largest NATO defense market' and build a defense industrial supply chain integrated with NATO standards, National Security Adviser Wi Sung-lac said. South Korea aims to become one of the world's top four defense-exporting nations and is competing with Germany for Canada's submarine procurement contract.

Lee will also meet NATO Secretary-General Mark Rutte and participate in a small-group session with leaders of NATO's four Indo-Pacific partners (Australia, Japan, New Zealand, South Korea). Meanwhile, an earlier headline noted that the Indian Army has selected the Israeli Meprolight MEPRO X6 as its new optic for Negev machine guns — another sign of how Middle Eastern and Asian defense suppliers are carving into traditional Western procurement channels.
sections.whyItMatters: South Korea's push into the NATO defense market is the most systematic effort by any non-member state to position itself as a credible alternative supplier for European rearmament. With European nations scrambling to fill capability gaps, Seoul's fast procurement cycles and competitive pricing give it a structural advantage — but only if it can meet NATO interoperability standards at scale. The Ankara summit will be a test of whether this ambition translates into contracts.

bottomLine: The dominant theme this week is the widening gap between defense ambitions and industrial capacity. GCAP's £4.6 billion contract shows one model working — a focused multinational partnership with a single prime contractor. But the KNDS IPO delay, NATO's reliance on improvised European backfills for US cutbacks, and Ukraine's desperate hunt for Patriot missiles all illustrate the strain on the existing system. Meanwhile, the MQ-28 Ghost Bat's first flight with an F-15EX in the Pacific offers a glimpse of a different future: one where manned-unmanned teaming reshapes air combat before sixth-generation fighters even enter service. South Korea's bid to enter the NATO supply chain as a top-tier exporter is another harbinger — the defense market is globalizing, and the incumbents are not moving fast enough to keep their seats.
sources: Reuters, UK Defence Journal, AP News, Euromaidan Press, The Aviationist, Korea Herald, War on the Rocks, Naval News, Hindustan Times, Anadolu Agency, Janes, MSN, Forbes, Kyiv Independent, Le Monde, Defense News
